video_title: The Next President May Control Superintelligence
gist: Encode founder Sneha Revanur walks through her AI-safety advocacy organization's playbook: the SB-1047-to-SB-53 arc in California, Illinois's third-party audit law, coalition-building against federal preemption, the 2025 OpenAI subpoena of her general counsel, and the money war between anti-regulation super PAC Leading the Future and an Anthropic-backed counter-PAC. Note on the title: despite the video's title referencing a future president controlling superintelligence, that theme is only a short segment near the end of a wider interview whose actual content is almost entirely about state-level AI legislation and advocacy tactics.

themes.theme: Founder's Shift From Near-Term AI Harms to Existential Risk

insights.claim: Sneha Revanur founded the AI-advocacy nonprofit Encode in 2020 at age 15 focused on near-term AI harms like criminal-justice algorithms, on the theory that AI was politically underpriced and would eventually become a major voting issue.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Shows AI policy advocacy can be seeded years ahead of an issue's political salience — worth watching which niche advocacy groups are positioning early for the next AI regulation fight.
insights.quote: I thought that AI was still very underpriced politically.

insights.claim: Revanur's core worry flipped from fearing AI would be overtrusted despite being unreliable (in contexts like criminal justice) to now believing society is underestimating current AI capabilities, a shift she attributes to daily hands-on use of ChatGPT rather than to any specific argument someone made to her.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Suggests direct product experience, not argumentation, is what converts AI-risk skeptics — relevant to predicting how fast elite and public opinion on AI risk can shift going forward.
insights.quote: what freaks me out more is that society is greatly underestimating and underpreparing for AI capabilities

insights.claim: Encode's core strategic philosophy in a fast-moving, uncertain AI-policy environment is to take many simultaneous, sometimes experimental political bets, since it's often impossible to know in advance which will succeed.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A template for how AI-safety advocacy orgs allocate scarce resources under deep uncertainty about AI timelines.
insights.quote: if you do everything, you will win

insights.claim: Encode intentionally owns the 'full stack' of AI policy work — from drafting bill language through post-passage implementation and enforcement staffing — rather than treating a bill's signing as the end goal.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Passage of an AI safety law isn't the finish line; track enforcement-office staffing and resourcing as the next leverage point in California and New York.

insights.claim: State-level AI regulators are structurally weaker than a federal regulator would be because states often lack the expertise and information-security infrastructure needed to handle sensitive data submitted by frontier AI labs.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A federal AI framework may eventually be needed for infosec/capacity reasons alone, independent of politics — relevant to how the state-vs-federal AI regulation debate resolves.

insights.claim: Coalitions opposing federal preemption of state AI laws span family-first religious conservatives, parents of children harmed by addictive AI companion apps, and Hollywood/Nashville performers worried about voice cloning — groups with otherwise unrelated political goals.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Cross-ideological coalitions are currently the main force blocking federal preemption of state AI laws — a leading indicator for whether preemption legislation passes Congress.

insights.claim: Revanur argues it's structurally easier to build a coalition against a shared threat, like federal preemption, than to unite the same groups behind a positive, affirmative policy agenda.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Explains why defensive coalitions (blocking preemption) currently outperform offensive ones (passing new federal AI safety law) — useful for predicting which side wins near-term legislative fights.

insights.claim: Encode maintains internal 'bright lines' enforced by general counsel Nathan Calvin, who screens every prospective bill for First Amendment/free-speech conflicts, to avoid coalition partners pulling the org toward positions it doesn't actually support.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A governance mechanism worth tracking: it caps how far Encode will go on kid-safety-style bills that could trade off against speech rights.

insights.claim: Encode considers itself in its 'third preemption rodeo,' and says multi-year trust built with legislative offices reduces friction and nitpicking when negotiating bill text — a relationship asset that's hard for new entrants to replicate quickly.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Relationship capital in AI policy advocacy compounds — later entrants to AI-safety advocacy face a steeper climb than Encode did.

insights.claim: The pro-preemption side — industry groups, the White House, and trade associations — has failed to unify around a single alternative federal AI framework, which Revanur cites as evidence their position is weaker than it appears.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Internal industry disunity on preemption is a reason to discount how close a federal preemption bill actually is to passing.

themes.theme: California/Illinois AI Law Track Record (SB-1047 to SB-53)

insights.claim: SB-1047, California's more aggressive 2024 frontier-AI safety bill, was vetoed by Governor Newsom, but it still built coalition infrastructure (labor unions, Hollywood actors) and prompted Newsom to convene an expert commission, including Fei-Fei Li and Tino Cuéllar, whose recommendations became the basis for the successor bill SB-53.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A vetoed bill can still be a durable policy investment — the 'failed' SB-1047 directly produced the successful SB-53 a year later.

insights.claim: SB-53, the California law that passed, requires frontier AI companies to publish safety and security plans and adds whistleblower protections, but it dropped SB-1047's third-party independent audit requirement and earlier proposed kill-switch/deployment restrictions.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Marks the current concrete regulatory floor for frontier labs operating in California — audits and kill-switches remain unresolved asks for future bills.

insights.claim: A specific negotiated win in SB-53 was extending its transparency requirements to cover internal, non-public deployments of frontier AI models, creating a secure state-government channel for lab employees to report risks from models that haven't been publicly released.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Closes a real regulatory gap since the most capable models often run internally before public release — creates the first regulatory visibility into pre-release model risk.

insights.claim: Illinois subsequently passed a third-party AI audit requirement that California's SB-53 lacks, which Revanur frames as a 'second-mover advantage': each new state's law raises the bar because later legislatures can point to an earlier state's law as precedent and political cover.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: State AI-safety standards are ratcheting upward, not settling at California's level — watch Illinois-style third-party-audit provisions propagate to other states next.

insights.claim: Revanur argues that had no state AI safety law passed at all, Congress would feel less pressure and could pass a weaker or entirely voluntary federal framework — the existence of state laws functions as a leverage floor in federal preemption negotiations.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: The state-law patchwork isn't just a stopgap — it's the mechanism setting the minimum bar any future federal AI law must clear.

themes.theme: The OpenAI Subpoena Episode and Its Aftermath

insights.claim: In 2025, during final negotiations over SB-53, OpenAI subpoenaed Encode's general counsel Nathan Calvin both individually (served at his home by a sheriff's deputy while he was having dinner) and at Encode's corporate address, after Encode had filed an amicus brief opposing OpenAI's for-profit restructuring in the Musk v. Altman lawsuit.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Shows frontier labs are willing to use aggressive legal tactics against advocacy nonprofits during live legislative fights — a tail risk for other small AI-safety orgs engaging labs.
insights.quote: pretty much had a sheriff's deputy show up to his house while he was having dinner with his wife and serve him the subpoena in real time.

insights.claim: Nathan Calvin's Twitter thread describing the subpoena went viral with 6 million views and prompted OpenAI chief strategy officer Jason Kwon to publicly respond, giving Encode a large jump in visibility within the AI policy world.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A single viral incident can be a bigger visibility and credibility event for a small advocacy nonprofit than years of prior lobbying work.

insights.claim: Despite the subpoena confrontation, Revanur deliberately avoided escalating publicly against OpenAI — calling their concerns about collusion 'reasonable' and praising the company's mission — specifically to preserve Encode's ability to keep negotiating with OpenAI's lobbyists in future state legislative fights.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Shows Encode treats OpenAI as a long-term repeat counterparty to be managed rather than a one-off adversary to be maximally punished — relevant to predicting future Encode-OpenAI dynamics.

insights.claim: Revanur attributes OpenAI's subsequent softer posture, including backing Illinois's third-party audit bill and calling for an international AI governance body, partly to internal employee pressure on the company's Global Affairs team led by Chris Lehane, not solely to external advocacy pressure.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Internal employee dissent inside frontier labs is an underappreciated lever shifting corporate AI-policy positions — worth tracking as its own signal.

themes.theme: Money in AI Politics: Super PACs and the Fairshake Playbook

insights.claim: Leading the Future, an anti-AI-regulation super PAC advised by Josh Vlasto, has raised $125 million for this year's midterms, making it the biggest spender of the cycle and AI regulation one of the most heavily funded single issues in American politics; a rival, Anthropic-backed counter-PAC has since emerged.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A concrete dollar figure and directional split ($125M anti-regulation vs. an Anthropic-backed counter-PAC) to track for how AI-regulation spending shapes the midterm primaries.

insights.claim: Revanur argues Silicon Valley overestimates that political capital is fully fungible into outcomes ('capital eats the world') — money swings low-salience races, like crypto where PAC Fairshake had roughly a 90% candidate success rate in 2024, but is far less decisive on high-salience issues where public sentiment is already mobilized, like AI.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Predicts anti-AI-regulation PAC spending will underperform the crypto-lobbying precedent because AI, unlike crypto, is becoming a salient issue voters already have formed opinions on.
insights.quote: there is this interesting misapprehension in Silicon Valley that capital eats the world and is fully fungible into political outcomes.

insights.claim: The same operatives built both the pro-crypto PAC Fairshake and the anti-AI-regulation PAC Leading the Future: OpenAI's Chris Lehane helped set up Fairshake after his time at Airbnb, and Leading the Future's Josh Vlasto previously advised Fairshake, suggesting they are reapplying a crypto-era political playbook to AI.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Because the same team runs both PACs, their AI political strategy can be read directly off the crypto playbook — useful for anticipating their next moves.
insights.quote: This is fundamentally not an AGI-pilled strategy.

insights.claim: Revanur claims some political candidates targeted by Leading the Future's attack ads have actually gained name recognition and fundraising as a result, leading her to describe anti-AI-regulation super PACs as increasingly a 'paper tiger.'
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: If attack spending is backfiring, advocates and investors should discount the near-term electoral threat from anti-regulation PAC money.
insights.quote: it actually seems like some of these super PACs that are spending big against regulation look more and more like a paper tiger day by day

insights.claim: AI is currently the fastest-growing issue in US political salience, but Revanur believes it hasn't yet reached the threshold where it swings individual votes, and she doesn't expect that threshold to be reached by this year's midterms.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Sets a testable timeline: watch whether AI becomes a vote-deciding issue before or after the midterms this cycle.

insights.claim: Political candidates are increasingly using AI as a differentiator in crowded primaries, including campaign ads that frame AI data centers as a political enemy and proposals like a 'token tax' on AI-driven job displacement.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: AI-adjacent physical infrastructure (data centers) and job-displacement policy (token taxes) are emerging as concrete midterm campaign flashpoints to watch.

insights.claim: Revanur expects the 2028 US presidential election could be one of the most consequential in history because its winner may end up overseeing or controlling an intelligence explosion or advanced-AI transition, regardless of whether AI timelines turn out to be short or long.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Frames the 2028 presidential race as itself a variable in AI-risk scenarios — worth tracking candidates' views on executive AI authority specifically, not just general AI policy.
insights.quote: this could either be the person who will be in charge of the intelligence explosion, or, in any case, this will be a person who is making some very major world-historic decisions about AI

insights.claim: Revanur is concerned the US executive branch will be the first branch of government to grasp how powerful AI is becoming, leaving the judiciary and Congress behind and concentrating AI-related decision-making power in the presidency.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Flags an under-discussed governance risk: unequal AI literacy across branches of government could become a power-concentration risk distinct from AI-company power.

insights.claim: Encode is prioritizing efforts to accelerate AI adoption specifically within the judicial and legislative branches, as a deliberate counterweight intended to prevent AI-related power from concentrating solely in the executive branch.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable Encode initiative — judicial and legislative AI adoption — that functions as a hedge against executive power concentration.

themes.theme: Sequencing Strategy for Harder Interventions: Liability and Warning Shots

insights.claim: Encode's sequencing strategy for tougher AI-safety interventions, like financial liability for AI harms or deployment restrictions, is to first codify practices that some labs are already doing voluntarily, like publishing safety plans, into law, then use that base to push more aggressive measures once political conditions allow.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Predicts the next wave of state AI bills will target liability and deployment restrictions only after safety-plan-style disclosure laws are broadly normalized.

insights.claim: Even using the word 'liability' was reportedly politically toxic during SB-1047 negotiations and drew significant industry opposition, despite the bill not actually proposing strict liability.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Liability-based AI regulation remains politically further out of reach than transparency and disclosure rules — a gauge for how far state legislatures are currently willing to go.

insights.claim: Revanur believes a 'warning shot' — most likely a disruptive AI-enabled cyberattack rather than a bioweapon incident, which she considers far less recoverable — is more likely than sustained argument to catalyze the political will needed for more aggressive AI safety interventions like liability.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A specific, watchable trigger — a major AI-enabled cyberattack — to monitor as a potential catalyst for a step-change in US AI regulation.
insights.quote: Hopefully it's not a really bad bioweapon, because that seems potentially a lot less recoverable

themes.theme: Career Advice for Aspiring AI Policy Advocates

insights.claim: Revanur describes effective AI policy advocacy as requiring the ability to switch between two conflicting mental modes — a persuasion-focused 'soldier' mode for coalition politics and a rigorous truth-seeking 'scout' mode drawn from AI-safety epistemic culture — warning that overdeveloping either skill erodes the other.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: A practical framework for evaluating AI-policy hires: look for people who can consciously toggle between persuasion and truth-seeking rather than defaulting to one mode.
insights.quote: you're kind of playing the role of a soldier — how do you also know when to be the scout

insights.claim: Revanur views AI safety advocacy as one of the most underresourced and talent-constrained ways to make AI go well, and notes the AI safety community is unusually flat and approachable compared to other industries, making it easy for newcomers to get mentorship.
insights.speaker: Sneha Revanur
insights.so_what: Signals a talent gap in AI policy advocacy careers — a data point for anyone assessing where marginal talent could have outsized impact in AI safety.
